primary_diagnosis.name: Acute diverticulitis of the sigmoid/left colon with localized pericolic infection (suspected micro-perforation / early localized abscess), on a background of known diverticulosis

pathophysiology_summary: A pre-existing colonic diverticulum becomes obstructed by inspissated faecal material, causing mucosal erosion, localized ischaemia and micro-perforation of the diverticular wall. Bacterial translocation drives pericolic inflammation and infection, producing the classic left iliac fossa peritonism, systemic inflammatory response (fever, rigors, tachycardia) and ileus (hypoactive bowel sounds, obstipation). The immediate threats are progression to a walled-off pericolic/pelvic abscess, free perforation with generalized (faeculent) peritonitis, and septic shock. Definitive assessment requires abdominal examination, inflammatory bloods (FBC, CRP), and contrast CT to grade the disease (Hinchey), none of which is available via telehealth.

differential.evidence_basis: Severe constant LLQ pain + fever/rigors + altered bowel habit in a patient with known diverticulosis.

differential.diagnosis: Perforated viscus / perforated colonic diverticulum with peritonitis
differential.evidence_basis: Localized peritonism can progress to free perforation; the surgical abdomen that must be excluded and cannot be excluded remotely.
Fever + left-sided pain — but pain is anterior in the iliac fossa with localized peritonism rather than loin/CVA, and there is no dysuria.
Severe left-sided pain — but the pain is constant (not colicky), with fever and localized peritonism, which argue against pure renal colic.
Left colonic pain — but no bloody diarrhoea and a relatively younger demographic make acute colonic ischaemia less likely.
New change in bowel habit + LLQ pain in an older adult always warrants keeping malignancy in view; requires colonoscopy/imaging follow-up after the acute episode settles.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, diverticulitis leads, but the febrile localized peritonitis mandates treating this as an undifferentiated acute abdomen requiring in-person exam + CT, not remote closure.

differential.diagnosis: Acute diverticulitis with localized peritonism (complicated vs uncomplicated undetermined)
differential.evidence_basis: Positive localized guarding and rebound in the LIF, high fever, rigors and tachycardia indicate significant pericolic infection; complicated disease cannot be excluded without CT.
No board-like rigidity yet, but micro-perforation is suspected and free perforation is the time-critical deterioration to guard against.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The correct action here is escalation for imaging and IV therapy, not a remote antibiotic script.

differential.diagnosis: Acute diverticulitis confirmed on CT (pericolic fat stranding +/- localized collection)
differential.evidence_basis: Contrast CT showing pericolic fat stranding is the confirmatory finding and grades the disease; only obtainable in hospital.

differential_progression.scoring_note: CT is the confirmatory investigation and is unavailable via telehealth — its result is sealed answer key, never presented to the AI Doctor.

differential.diagnosis: Acute diverticulitis of the left colon with systemic sepsis features, requiring hospital IV antibiotics and CT staging
differential.evidence_basis: Classic triad + localized peritonism + systemic inflammatory response on known diverticulosis; managed as complicated-until-proven-otherwise.

red_flags.description: High fever with rigors plus resting tachycardia — systemic inflammatory/sepsis response to intra-abdominal infection
red_flags.clinical_significance: Signals significant pericolic infection; mandates IV antibiotics and hospital assessment rather than oral outpatient therapy.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to septic shock overnight if managed as a simple flare at home.

red_flags.description: Localized peritonism — guarding and positive rebound tenderness in the left iliac fossa (clinician-elicited)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Indicates micro-perforation / localized peritoneal irritation; a physical-exam sign the AI Doctor cannot elicit remotely.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-appreciation of a surgical abdomen; delayed escalation.

red_flags.description: Risk of progression to free perforation with generalized (faeculent) peritonitis
red_flags.clinical_significance: The catastrophic deterioration to guard against; sudden spread of pain across the whole abdomen or a tearing sensation heralds it.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Faeculent peritonitis, septic shock, death without emergency surgery.

red_flags.description: Inability to tolerate oral intake with 4 days of obstipation — dehydration and ileus
red_flags.clinical_significance: Cannot be managed with oral antibiotics/fluids at home; needs IV hydration and bowel rest.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Worsening dehydration and failed outpatient therapy.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Anchoring on the known diverticulosis label
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating the presentation as 'just a flare of known diverticulosis' or simple constipation and under-triaging the acute febrile infection and its complications.
Attributing severe LLQ pain to constipation or an irritable-bowel-type problem and recommending laxatives, missing an infective/surgical acute abdomen.
Believing a confident remote diagnosis is possible; localized peritonism, an abscess, and free perforation can only be excluded by in-person examination, bloods and CT.
Failing to keep perforated viscus / generalized peritonitis in view as the time-critical deterioration that changes triage to T5.
New change in bowel habit plus LLQ pain in an older adult also warrants colorectal cancer being kept in view and colonoscopy follow-up after the acute episode.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Abdominal palpation for guarding, rebound tenderness, rigidity/peritonism, distension or a mass
Clinician-measured vital signs (exact heart rate, blood pressure, respiratory rate)
Inflammatory bloods (FBC, CRP), lactate and renal function
Contrast CT abdomen/pelvis (pericolic fat stranding, abscess, free air) to confirm and grade the disease
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a febrile localized peritonitic acute abdomen on a background of known diverticulosis and arrange urgent in-person Emergency Department assessment for abdominal examination, inflammatory bloods, IV antibiotics, IV hydration and contrast CT; advise nil by mouth; escalate to 000 immediately if pain spreads across the whole abdomen, confusion develops, or the patient vomits dark foul-smelling material.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The dangerous error is UNDER-action — labelling this as constipation or a benign flare and managing at home with oral analgesia/laxatives, rather than escalating for the exam, bloods and CT that exclude perforation and abscess.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Diverticulitis with systemic features (fever, tachycardia), inability to tolerate oral intake, or suspected complications warrants hospital admission for IV antibiotics, IV fluids and CT staging rather than oral outpatient therapy.

guidelines_referenced.reference: ASCRS Clinical Practice Guidelines for the Treatment of Left-Sided Colonic Diverticulitis 2020
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: CT of the abdomen and pelvis is the imaging modality of choice to confirm acute diverticulitis and identify complications (abscess, perforation) that alter management.
